_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Martin County, NC. Record survival, courthouse history. Williamston courthouse.
display_name: Martin County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced over time. Records transferred to subsequent buildings.

record_losses.description: A fire at the Martin County courthouse in 1884 destroyed some records, particularly loose papers and early court minutes. The bound deed books and most will books survived — they were stored in a vault that withstood the fire. The 1885 courthouse was built as the replacement. The fire means that some pre-1830 court records are lost.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The 1884 fire destroyed some early records, making pre-1830 research in Martin County more difficult. However, the deed books survived, and for {ancestor_name}, land transactions may be the most reliable surviving trail. The will books are mostly intact but with some gaps.
record_losses.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh, 2001.

deeds.completeness: Good — deed books survived the 1884 fire in the vault. Continuous from county formation.
deeds.gaps: No known gaps in deed books.
deeds.repository: Martin County Register of Deeds, Williamston; NC State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com NC land records

wills_estates.completeness: Partial — most will books survived the fire but some loose estate papers were lost
wills_estates.gaps: Loose estate papers from the pre-1830 period are fragmentary. Bound will books generally intact.
wills_estates.repository: Martin County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds survived the fire
marriages.repository: Martin County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Partial — court minutes before 1830 were destroyed in the 1884 fire. Post-1830 minutes generally complete.
court_minutes.gaps: No court minutes survive before approximately 1830.
court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; Martin County Clerk of Court

vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790-1940. Martin County enumerated from the first federal census.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families, particularly before 1830 when court minutes are lost. Census records may be the only surviving documentation for some early families.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives. Federal Census Schedules, Martin County, NC.

substitute_sources.description: Martin County appears in the Colonial Records and State Records of North Carolina for the pre-1830 period, providing tax lists, militia rolls, and legislative references that supplement the lost court minutes.
substitute_sources.repository: NC State Archives; published volumes (Saunders and Clark editions)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Tax lists and militia rolls from the Colonial/State Records series can substitute for the lost court minutes in identifying family heads and assessing economic status.
substitute_sources.source: Clark, Walter, ed. The State Records of North Carolina. 16 vols. Winston and Goldsboro: State of NC, 1895-1906.

substitute_sources.description: Baptist and Methodist congregations in Martin County maintained membership rolls and minutes. Church records may predate the courthouse fire's destruction threshold.
substitute_sources.repository: NC Baptist Historical Collection, Wake Forest University; individual churches; NC State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Church records are essential substitutes for the lost pre-1830 court records. Membership rolls, disciplinary proceedings, and letters of dismissal document family movements.
substitute_sources.source: Paschal, George W. History of North Carolina Baptists. Raleigh: NC Baptist State Convention, 1930.

narrative_hooks: The 1884 fire at the Martin County courthouse destroyed some early records — but the deed books survived in the vault, and for {ancestor_name}, the land record may be the most reliable trail before 1830.

Martin County's court minutes before 1830 were lost in the 1884 fire, so for {ancestor_name}'s earliest generation, the substitute sources are census records, tax lists from the State Records, and whatever the church recorded.
If {ancestor_name} bought or sold land in Martin County, the deed almost certainly survives — the deed books were protected by the vault when the courthouse burned.

Before 1913, no North Carolina county required birth or death registration. In Martin County, where the courthouse fire also destroyed some early records, the census and church records are the last resort for {ancestor_name}'s vital dates.
Martin County's marriage bonds survive from 1774 — if {ancestor_name} married in this county, the bond naming both parties is likely still in the vault at Williamston.
source_references: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
Clark, Walter, ed. The State Records of North Carolina. 16 vols. Winston and Goldsboro: State of NC, 1895-1906.
Paschal, George W. History of North Carolina Baptists. Raleigh: NC Baptist State Convention, 1930.
National Archives. Federal Census Schedules, Martin County, NC.
Tilley, Nannie May. The Bright-Tobacco Industry, 1860-1929. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1948.
